failure_surface: Code execution consistently fails with SyntaxError indicating non-code characters (tool markers) appearing at line 1 of the Python script.
root_cause: The agent's written code file became corrupted with tool invocation markers or other non-Python characters during the write-to-file process, causing immediate execution failure before any spreadsheet manipulation logic could run.
minimal_fix_hypothesis: Before executing generated code, verify the target file contains only valid Python syntax without extraneous characters by reading and inspecting its contents post-write.
evidence: Multiple execution attempts all produced 'Cell In[1], line 1 | <tool_call> | ^ SyntaxError: invalid syntax'
The <tool_call> character is a tool invocation marker, not valid Python syntax
Agent wrote code via write_file successfully but execution environment showed corrupted content
No successful execution log showing data transformation output (no 'Found X entries' print statements completed)

memory_items.title: Execute-Write Verification
memory_items.lesson: After writing code to a file, always read and validate the file contents before execution to ensure no corruption occurred.
memory_items.generalization: In multi-step workflows involving file I/O followed by execution, add an intermediate verification step to catch write-process contamination before runtime.

memory_items.title: Tool Marker Contamination Prevention
memory_items.lesson: Be aware that certain execution environments may inadvertently include tool invocation syntax in output files if boundaries are not properly managed.
memory_items.generalization: When generating executable scripts through tool chains, ensure clean separation between tool control syntax and file payload content.

memory_items.title: Early-Exit Detection Strategy
memory_items.lesson: Syntax errors occurring at line 1 typically indicate file-level corruption rather than logic errors; diagnose environment/tool-chain issues before debugging algorithmic logic.
memory_items.generalization: Categorize initial execution failures by error type: file-level syntax issues require infrastructure fixes, while deeper errors require logic adjustments.

edits.content:
## Execution Hygiene
### Code Write Verification
After writing any executable script file, always read the file contents back and confirm:
1. No extraneous characters appear at the beginning or within the file
2. First line is valid import or Python statement
3. File encoding is clean UTF-8 without unexpected bytes

Only proceed to execution after verification passes.

### Syntax Validation Before Runtime
Run a lightweight syntax check (e.g., `python -m py_compile <file>`) before full execution to catch corruption issues early.

### Tool Boundary Awareness
Ensure tool invocation markers, metadata, or control syntax cannot leak into the actual file payload during write operations.
